And actually, I'm planning to do dsa from scratch in rust. I want to "really" learn rust and I think doing dsa in a specific language really teaches you many in and outs of a language
I know many languages, python cpp rust go js ts but none as good as cpp
You basically have to utilise all of the language to do dsa
Basically I argue about rust being a good programming language for beginners because even if they don't know what RAII is, they will be forced to do good memory management
In c++ they will throw a few dangling pointers
And call it a day
In rust you can setup clippy to ban function calls
Such as unwrap and expect
To me, rust looks like a different approach all together than traditional languages
All the time, either we free memory or the gc does
But now, memory is freed by rules
But having prior low level knowledge helps a lot

That's why rust feels so good coming from cpp and I want to really learn it. Not for job, not for a specific thing but I'm just interested in the concept. 
I know the basics but I want to be able to read and understand open source code and for that I would require deep understanding

In the end it's a c-like that choses to not allocate to the heap unless explicitly stated, and when allocated and the structure gets destroyed, free is called on the Box pointer, not because of intrinsics really, just because it calls free on the destructor itself

next js does provide their "Image" component for optimizing images on their servers but that would mean a lot of money for me if I provide this as a service

But I do need to optimize images because I can't just let the page load 100 images each 3-4MB for a 100x100 preview and multiple kind of previews.

I was thinking of following approaches:
1. If I ask the user to only upload via my service, then I can make thumbnails myself and store them in a .thumbnails folder in s3. But if the user doesn't do that, then I'll have to render the full res image for each preview
2. I go for an external optimizer like cloudfront + lambda. It will be cheaper than next Image but still can cost a lot for high amount of images
3. For now, I'm receiving an array of image urls on frontend. I'll use server action to convert all those urls to blobs and then perform client-side libraries like sharp to resize them for usecases. But in this approach, firstly a lot of time will be taken to get all blobs + all that processing on client.
I'd make thumbnails and cache on redis
Store metadata and thumbails in hashes
so 1st approach but redis instead of their buckets?
but what happens if i restart the backend instance
all the thumbnails gone and no way to restore them on backend than creating thumbnails on get request which would require me to get all the blobs
You can render the downscaled images yourself
On first upload simply make an async task that downscales the image
There is not only libraries for it
You can also do it yourself
Store that in a previews hidden folder in your bucket
Tie it with metadata in the database or implicitly by name
so just 1st approach
but no database will be involved, I'm not storing any files related data in database
Or instead of a hidden folder do add a ._image.preview.format
You'd need to check file ownership and permissions
that's the safest option for me because that would mean no charge for me

Ask manware

``` Gemini said

Your solution is clever, but it fails on cases where closing brackets appear out of order or where opening brackets are incorrectly popped.

Because your code currently says "if it doesn't match, just append it," you end up treating closing brackets as opening brackets if they don't find an immediate partner.
The "False Match" Example

Consider the input string: s = "((]"

Here is how your code processes it:

    Start: stack = ["$"]

    Iterate (: brackets.get('(', 0) is 0. 0 != "$".

        Action: stack.append('(') → stack = ["$", "("]

    Iterate (: brackets.get('(', 0) is 0. 0 != "(".

        Action: stack.append('(') → stack = ["$", "(", "("]

    Iterate ]: brackets.get(']', 0) is '['. [ does not match the top of the stack (.

        Action: stack.append(']') → stack = ["$", "(", "(", "]"]

The Result: The loop ends, stack[-1] is "]", so it returns False. This specific case actually "passes" by failing, but it doesn't follow the rules of a stack.```
